Regarding `@signalled` (point 1): This is up to the developer, because `@dbus_property` generates a normal `@property` so using `@foobar.setter` will work and you don't have to use `@signalled`. It is only there so that it automatically populates that XML entry.

And regarding the weakly typed methods and properties (point 3), `@dbus_property` and `@dbus_method` still provide the functionality of defining/overriding the interface used.

Also `gen_introspection` already makes it possible to use it like you suggested by explicitly doing `clazz.dbus = clazz…`.

The issue is that the windows console does not interpret the 'arrow keys' as ansi codes, like unix terminals, but rather as virtual keystrokes. The virtual keystrokes are captured by the parent process, and not translated into ansi codes or passed through to the child process windows event loop. The only way to do this is with a windows event hook dll, which translates the virtual keystrokes from the parent console and passes them to the child process event loop. This is what conemu is doing, and every similar subshell under Win32 would have to do. I'm not suggesting this is worth anyone's time, since the workaround (using conemu) is possibly acceptable.

That said, I'm not sure you'd even want this fix if i were to write it. It would involve compiling a binary that gets injected into every window on the system, which is what conemu is doing. One might be able to do it in a less invasive fashion, but it would be some pretty deep win32. I'm willing to give it a shot at some point, unless someone here really wants a go at it.

I mean some ideas of an API I have been playing with.

```
async def child():
    await sleep(5)
    raise Exception("woops")

async def parent():
    task = await spawn(child())
    await link(task)
    task, exception = await monitor()
```

Where `monitor` will just schedule the parent until there are no linked tasks running OR one of those linked tasks has failed with an exception.

Maybe this could be handled in userland like how `curio.wait` works

I decided to implement the copyto tag as I realized that it actually solves a few other problems as well, so I think it's good to have. In your case it can be used for putting data files directly under the work root, and you can also use it to copy xci files to different directoreies under the work root and use them from there, so that you avoid messing up files in your source repos.

[0001-Add-Xilinx-Vivado-backend-and-rework-XSIM.patch.txt](https://github.com/olofk/fusesoc/files/1367143/0001-Add-Xilinx-Vivado-backend-and-rework-XSIM.patch.txt)

I still think we need a proper xci parser though, so that we can use the generated HDL files in other simulators that don't handle xci natively. This is actually implemented already in the logicore provider. It isn't very obvious, has some shortcomings and is also not very documented, but you can find some info in my [blog post about FuseSoC 1.5](olofkindgren.blogspot.com/2016/08/fusesoc-15.html)

Eventually, I would like to have a stand-alone xci parser that can be part of the not-yet-existant generator framework, but we need to find a good solution in the near-time

Thanks for the review/comments @arokem!  Some responses:

> Why are tests in the top-level directory, rather than under the package?

I suppose this is a habit I picked up from developing packages with large regression test dependencies.  More generally, I prefer to keep tests separate from the package so that the tests operate in an environment that best matches application code (ie, package code is loaded by absolute import instead of relative).

This issue came up previously in building deb packages #36.  I'm open to moving the tests in this package, but haven't yet done so.



> Having said that, even after building the package, I get the following test errors (maybe I'm doing it wrong?):

The tests are written for `py.test`, not `nosetests`, so that probably explains the errors you're getting.

I guess this means the documentation should be expanded to include running tests. :grin:

In general, the daemon should not use `sys.exit` at all, since it's a forked child of the original control process and hence should use [`os._exit`](https://docs.python.org/2.7/library/os.html#os._exit) instead. However, calling `os._exit` from within the daemon's `run` method isn't a good idea, either, since that (by definition) immediately exits without any chance for cleanup.

The intended way of shutting down the daemon from within `run` is to simply `return`. That should be documented.

In addition, handling a call to `sys.exit` in `run` correctly would be rather easy, all we have to do is to catch the corresponding [`SystemExit`](https://docs.python.org/2/library/exceptions.html#exceptions.SystemExit) exception, clean up, and exit.

In the meantime, you can do that yourself, @fedus:

```python
def run(self):
    try:
        # Your usual daemon code goes here
    except SystemExit:
        # Swallow exception from sys.exit so that we can clean up
        return
```

hello,
for the first problem , you don't have to resend the keyboard if it is not changed , so you can omit the message when not sending a new keyboard. and you can bring back the main keyboard in the same message that notifies the user a submenu item is pressed.

also, I would like to mention that the whole `handler` function is executed each time a message is sent to your bot. 

as for second problem (the nested if) you have a logical problem , when you nest two if's both conditions must be true (i.e. they are AND-ed) so this is your condition:
```python
if chat_message == 'Option #1' and chat_message == 'Sub option #1_1':
```
which is always false because it can't be both at the same time ...

you should either switch to a delegate bot and keep state of your bot (which keyboard is sent) in a user-specific variable and use that variable in your conditions; or if you don't need keys in different menus to have the same text you can try this code:

```python
import telepot

def handler(msg):
    chat_id = msg['chat']['id']
    chat_message = msg['text']
    
    main_keyboard = [['Option #1', 'Option #2'], ['Option #3', 'Option #4']]
    sub_keyboard = [['Sub option #1_1', 'Sub option #1_2'], ['Sub option #1_3', 'Sub option #1_4'],['Back to Main menu']]
    
    if chat_message=='/start':
        bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Main options', reply_markup={'keyboard': main_keyboard})
    elif chat_message in [j for i in main_keyboard for j in i]:
        # an option from Main keyboard is chosen:
        
        # Ex: Option #1 > You selected Option #1
        bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Main selected: %s' %chat_message)
        
        if chat_message == 'Option #1':
            sub_buttons = {'keyboard': sub_keyboard}
            bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Sub options', reply_markup=sub_buttons)
 
    elif chat_message in [j for i in sub_keyboard for j in i]:
        # an option from Sub keyboard is chosen:
        if chat_message == 'Sub option #1_1':
            bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Sub selected %s' %chat_message)
        if chat_message == 'Back to Main menu':
            bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Main options', reply_markup={'keyboard': main_keyboard})
            
    else:
        bot.sendMessage(chat_id, 'Invalid Message. please select an option from keyboard')
 
bot = telepot.Bot('191891323:AAHb42-2xFjUWc0M0dwx8CzafJUbUIuQUas')
print('[+] started...')
bot.message_loop(handler, run_forever=True)
```

Merged to master.

Both routes and interfaces transactions now have a uniform API and behaviour. New virtual fields:
* `ipdb_scope` — can be:
  * `create` — just created and not yet reflected on the OS level
  * `system` — reflected on the OS level
  * `shadow` — an object should stay in the DB, but should be removed from the OS
  * `remove` — an object should be deleted
  * `locked` — an object is going to be deleted
* `ipdb_priority` — transaction priority to be used when committed on the IPDB level; the higher, the earlier the transaction will be committed

Docs are coming soon.

Done on master.  Rlens and Rperp metrics are allowed to give min_rpar, max_rpar parameters which limit the minimum and maximum value of r_source - r_lens, where "source" is the second catalog of the two, and "lens" is the first catalog.  

This is a signed quantity, so to specify that you only want background galaxies, you can use `min_rpar = 0`.  To specify that you only want local galaxies (for II or halo studies) you can use e.g. `min_rpar = -50` and `max_rpar = 50`.

One caveat is that I don't use the min/max rpar values for splitting, just for pruning pairs of cells.  This means that if you want a fairly exact rpar cutoff, you might need to use a lower than normal bin_slop to make sure that the regular splitting process gets down to small enough cells that the error in rpar is small.  OTOH, I suspect most people won't need the rpar cutoff to be extremely precise, so I think this is fine.

The usual recommendation about bin_slop applies: Check if using a bin_slop half as large makes any significant difference to the statistic you are making, and if not, then you're fine.  If it makes a difference, you probably need to go smaller.

Hi, yes this is a problem with the current command line interface. The individual plot types can add additional arguments, in this case, the "histogram" type adds the `-b` or `--bucketsize` parameter. Unfortunately, the top-level parser isn't aware of the additional argument and because it must consume one required argument (the filename) it uses the first it is unaware of: the `1`. 

To avoid this, you can either pipe the file in like you do, or you can put the filename at the first place directly after the command: 

```
mplotqueries mtools_filt.log --type histogram -b 1
```

This is a bit unusual for Linux users, who are used to adding the required argument last, but it gets around the issue. 

I've been meaning to make mplotqueries sub-command based, like for example git, see #348, which would fix the problem and make for a nice interface. The command would then become

```
mplotqueries histogram -b 1 mtools_fit.log
mplotqueries connchurn ...
mplotqueries scatter ...
```

Unfortunately I'm just a little short on time to introduce big changes like that.

@jamiebull1 
You have been working in tricky part of the code. Also you have run into a nasty problem that unit testing has. Most people discover it by running into it.

Unit test does not play well with reading or writing files on disk. Once you set a path name, you find you cannot run the unit test from other folders. When your write tests fail, it leaves files scattered on the disk. Since the test failed, the cleanup is not done. The test code also winds up being very fragile. You cannot move files or folders around, Everything breaks
    
The standard solution to this is not use file name, but to use file handles in the functions you are testing. 
    
So you would not write the following function:

    def readafile(filename):
        return open(filename, 'r').read()
        
instead you would write:

    def readafilehandle(fhandle):
        return fhandle.read()

    # write unit test for readafilehandle
    def test_readafilehandle():
       import StringIO
       txt = 'hello'
       fhandle = StringIO.StringIO(txt)
       assert readafilehandle(fhandle) == 'hello'

    fhandle = open(filename, 'r')
    txt = readafilehandle(fhandle)
        
Often eppy's code does not cooperate with this kind of unit testing.
So when I write unit tests, I have wind up rewriting the functions so that they use file handles. But there are times you really need functions that use filename :-(

I have not found any good solutions for unit testing actual reads and writes to the disk. I have some ideas on how to do it without making the tests too fragile, but they are kind of ugly.

Sorry I did not flag you on it early enough. I had forgotten what a problem this was with unit test. If you google "python unit tests and IO", you will see others complaining about this.

You could try sorting twice.  The first time, use `natsort` with `LOCALE`, and then the second time use python's built in sort with the `itemgetter` function from the `operator` module to sort on the first letter using ordinal sorting.  Because sorting is guaranteed to be stable in python, within each letter your order will be maintained, and the order of the first letters will be what you want.

```python
>>> from natsort import natsorted, ns
>>> from operator import itemgetter
>>> a = ['Apple',  'c1', 'Banana', 'apple',  'c14', 'banana', 'C4', 'Apples', 'C30', 'Applu','c2']
>>> sorted(natsorted(a, alg=ns.LOCALE), key=itemgetter(0))
>>> ['Apple', 'Apples', 'Applu', 'Banana', 'C4', 'C30', 'apple', 'banana', 'c1', 'c2', 'c14']
```

I realize this may not be desirable since you will have to sort twice, but I don't believe I could support this within the `natsort` library.

If we assume that `pos` is still a `Position` instance, then the logical conclusion is that something has overwritten the `size` member attribute with `numpy.bool_` and apparently a `self.size != 0` comparison returns a `numpy.bool_` rather than a `bool`

Each "order" carries a "data" attribute which is obviously the data for which the order has been created.

  - data._name gives you access to the name

"_name" may be set by the end user when adding the data

  - cerebro.adddata(mydata, name="xxxx")

If not set some datas will try to set it using a reasonable default. For example:

  - CSV derived subclasses will use the filename (not the entire path, just the filename) as the data name

Positions do not have a "data" because you actually retrieve the position using "data" as the key from inside the strategy. The "getposition" method is defined as:

  - def getposition(self, data=None, broker=None)

If "data" is None then data0 (the 1st data added to the system) will be used as the key for the returned position (given most people only test with a single data, it seems a reasonable default)

Looking at the change too, I think the change in the `ietf-json-serialise` tests that you've made isn't valid according to RFC7951. The `empty` type should be serialised to be `[null]` rather than simply `null`: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7951#section-6.9 -- i.e., the original JSON output is correct. This is actually where the test above is failing.

It also looks like there isn't a test for deserialise - and the code here fails (the `set_method` is called on `d[key]` rather than the rewritten value).

A patch is released whenever you are fixing a bug or incorrect behavior, with that fix being backward compatible. It has nothing to do with the difference between newer versions. In your example, when you have released 1.0.0, and you have NOT released 1.1.0, and you have to fix something you still would release 1.0.1. You might confuse it with `backporting`, which happens when a new feature is introduced into older versions in a backwards compatible way? Patching really just means fixing anything that is wrong,buggy, incorrect behavior. No other meanings are implied.

I didn't think about the proper fix yet, but I can give you some ideas. The error happens because we explicitly set autocommit to True which is needed for SQLAlchemy, because I didn't find any other way to make it execute custom raw SQL statements using cursor object, but as you see this pisses off Django ORM.

The simplest of all possible fixes would be to just pass the flag like what ORM we're using and create a series of IF statements inside which we could turn autocommit to on or off, depending on the ORM being used, but this is a quick and dirty fix, because it hardcodes the ORM's and I really don't like it.

We should create something more abstract, something more object oriented without hardcoding. But this solution needs to be very well thought, and unfortunately as I said I don't have time to think about it at the moment.

As for the somebody new on the project, why not, but at first he/she needs to make several pull requests and I have to see that this pull requests are really smart and we think in the same direction when working on problem solving tasks.

BTW I believe you can use django-db-parti package which doesn't have this problem and when this error will be fixed in architect you can safely switch to it because it is backwards compatible with django-db-parti.

Hi @mathandy,

I was mostly concerned with the style attribute, because it requires full CSS parsing to resolve the inheritances. A group can have style properties, that the path overwrites but the others also apply to the path. Also there is style classes which can be defined for groups and path elements, that are also inherited according to rules. And last but not least, there is inline stylesheets in SVG that can address specific elements (groups or paths) by their respective ID and change their style.

For SVG 2 it is required for the user agent to parse the CSS. In the [SVG 1.1 documentation](https://www.w3.org/TR/SVG/styling.html) I found the hint that the CSS cascade must be obeyed if CSS is parsed (this was optional for SVG 1.1). The inheritance rules for CSS are given in chapter 6 of the the [CSS documentation](https://www.w3.org/TR/2008/REC-CSS2-20080411/). Those can get really complicated and this fact alone was reason for me not to deal with inheritance at all. But maybe there is some Python code for CSS parsing around, you can just import as a dependency...

And another thought: I would intuitively say that clip-paths are inherited similar to transformations so that all of them apply to the path in the respective order...


Cheers

No.  Just setting a small sample rate generates too many unnecessary segments.  This is a big problem where the nurbs are very flat and straight and your CNC machine chokes on these points when it is built to run smoothly along what is a straight line. 

As I said, the optimum for CAM (be it laser cutting or machining) is as few straight segments as possible, but within the deviation tolerance of the desired curve.  

A cheap way to implement this is to oversample the curve at a high sample rate, and then delete points that are almost colinear with their neighbours until the curve moves too far away from the original.  

However, if you have the maths right in terms of understanding the curvature of the nurbs, you can directly calculate the deflection of the curve from the straight line segment between two sample points, and therefore produce exactly the right sample rate along any nurbs curve to maintain tolerance for the resulting piecewise linear approximation.

Instead of adding `object.__post_init__`, I think I could generate code to call `super.__post_init__` only if it exists. But as always the problem with cooperative multiple inheritance is knowing what parameters to call the `super` method it with, since with this change we've added params to `__post_init__`.

I think we had a similar discussion with chaining `__init__` calls and decided to leave it up to the user to work it out: we don't call `super.__init__` at all. I'd like to do the same here: if you the class author understand the `__post_init__` hierarchy enough, just chain the calls yourself with the right params, because we can't get it right.

That said, I think we could inspect `super.__post_init__` and find the parameter names. Since the parameter names aren't arbitrary (they're the names of the `InitVar` fields, after all), we could then build up the list of params and call the method with the right values. But that seems kind of extreme.

I am inclined more towards the factory idiom, it does not actually look too verbose:
```python
class Example:
    shared_names: List[str] = []
    keys: List[int] = field(factory=list)
```
However I am not sure that we need to raise an exception for the first field above. Potentially, we can still allow plain lists, sets, dicts for class variables:
```python
   shared: ClassVar[List[str]] = [] # This can be OK (if we not include this in __init__)
```
however this will require importing ``typing`` which may induce some speed penalty.

Sure! Formatter is constructed in `_make_handlers` before each testcase. Information about format is hardcoded in three places:

a. FORMAT variable:
```
def _make_handlers(stdoutloggers, fileloggers, item):
    FORMAT = '%(asctime)s %(levelshortname)s %(name)s: %(message)s'
    fmt = Formatter(fmt=FORMAT)
```
b. asctime: Formatter (derived class) override for formatTime
```
class Formatter(logging.Formatter):
# ...
    def formatTime(self, record, datefmt=None):
        ct = record.created - self._start
        dt = datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(ct)
        return dt.strftime("%M:%S.%f")[:-3]  # omit useconds, leave mseconds
```
c. levelshortname: additional non-standard member of record:
```
class Formatter(logging.Formatter):
# ...
    def format(self, record):
        record.levelshortname = Formatter.short_level_names.get(record.levelno,
                                                                'l%s' % record.levelno)
```

If you'd like to modify only #a, you could:
- add relevant API to LoggerConfig (e.g. set_format) and store user's format,
- pass format from LoggerPlugin.pytest_runtest_setup to LoggerState object ctor...
- ...which in turn passes it to _make_handlers.

2. You need to show your example code here, including the version of exchangelib you tested with.

3. The `response_type` is apparently only available on the calendar item of the organizer. See https://github.com/ecederstrand/exchangelib/issues/362

4. Again, please post your code. It's also possible that the colors are not, in fact, provided by categories, but some extended property instead.

I had the same issue and fixed it by using S3Boto3Storage instead of S3BotoStorage and two not documented parameters in my settings.py.

requirements.txt:
 * boto3==1.4.1
 * django-storages==1.5.1

settings.py:
 * STATICFILES_STORAGE = 'storages.backends.s3boto3.S3Boto3Storage'
 * AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = '<my access key>'
 * AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = '<my secret key>'
 * AWS_STORAGE_BUCKET_NAME = '<my bucket name>'
 * AWS_S3_REGION_NAME = 'eu-central-1'    (not documented)
 * AWS_S3_SIGNATURE_VERSION = 's3v4'    (not documented)
 * AWS_QUERYSTRING_AUTH = False
 * AWS_S3_FILE_OVERWRITE = True

To be honest, I didn't set the STATICFILES_STORAGE setting, because my application uses the S3Boto3Storage class for specific tasks, not for all static files. But I think it should work this way.

I stumbled upon the not documented options by going through the source code of django-storages.

Hope this will be a solution for others too.

We *could* look at `tox.ini` and not complain about a test dependency when it is installed there, but then you would need a separate `tox.ini` dependency checker: maybe stuff gets installed in tox that is no longer needed.

Sorry, but I don't like and don't want to follow PEP8.

As should be obvious from the code here, I kinda have my own weird and crappy style, and I've tried adopting PEP8 in the past, but all it led to is me abandoning the codebase, as it's too hard and inconvenient to work with (e.g. pulseaudio-mixer-cli projects, and some others) or refactoring back to my style (e.g. python-onedrive).

I guess many people might be fine with it though, likely the majority, so I think refactoring you did and stuff such as tests that you're planning to do is awesome and The Right Thing for a module useful for everybody, it's just that I'd rather maintain my own fork - way easier for me that way.

Furthermore, people asked for things like Python 2.6 and 3.x here a lot (iirc, check closed issues), which I'm also just "meh" about, and if you'll also be willing to implement these, I think it'll be fantastic.

------

So going forward, I think maintaining a separate pep8 and "best practices" fork is still the best option.

As I can't vouch for any fork (no offense), I'd rather not transfer PyPI project to fork that I don't know much about, as that can be unfair to any (probably super-rare) users of the project, especially if there are any significant changes planned there.

But given that the name of this project is kinda terrible anyway (as with most of my projects, blame lack of imagination), I think you can just come up with the better one and register that on PyPI instead, and I propose to link it at the top of the README with "FOR NEW PROJECTS USE THAT ONE INSTEAD" kind of message.

You can also send message to github support and they'll make your fork not to be considered such (i.e. no "forked from ..." at the top), which I think might be a good idea with a separate pypi project - to avoid confusion for new users as to which "fork" to pick, and some other upsides github-ui-wise.

```
envlist =
```

What you linked is a description of how to manipulate features of the DynamoDBMapper Java library in order to use DynamoDB condition expressions[1]. All methods in boto3 DynamoDB clients that write data to DynamoDB accept a `ConditionExpression` parameter through which you can control this behavior directly.

For example, if you wanted to want to replicate the behavior of the example in the DynamoDBMapper example using the boto3 Table resource, it would look something like this:

```python
import boto3
from boto3.dynamodb.conditions import Attr

previous_version = 8
item = {
    'Id': '...',
    'Title': '...',
    'ISBN': '...',
    'Authors': ['...', '...'],
    'version': 9
}

table = boto3.resource('dynamodb').Table('ProductCatalog')
table.put_item(
    Item=item,
    # Only write the item if the existing item in the table has the previous version.
    ConditionExpression=Attr('version').eq(previous_version)
)
```

[1] https://docs.aws.amazon.com/amazondynamodb/latest/developerguide/Expressions.ConditionExpressions.html

This is just the nature of how equality works for resources, where equality is determined by the equality of identifiers. The assumption with identifiers is that a specific set of identifiers will map to a unique AWS resource and thus can be used to check equality, but it looks like that is not the case for Metrics. We will have to look into what can be done, but unfortunately adding dimension as an identifier would be a breaking change so we would not be able to do that.

Multiplying a 200x1 vector and another 200x1 vector is not a valid operation. You'll need to either take the transpose of the first (i.e. dot product), or the transpose of the second (i.e. Kronecker product). Okay, so recently I realized that you are using the predictions from network directly and run validation using another evaluation function. I forget to mention that the results are based on `data_shape` of the network, i.e. 512 for 512 networks, 300 for 300 networks, rather than original image resolutions.
So you will need to rescale the bounding boxes before using official or other metrics.

In gluoncv, we use scaled predictions and scaled ground-truth at the same time to avoid rescale predictions.

Keep in mind from https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/kivy-users/mgisdssF39M/28vgOWfM0y0J that:

> There are 2 locations for blacklist.txt, one is inside .buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/src/ and the other is in python-for-android/dist/distname, the first changes the blacklist for every compilation the other for a specific compilation.

So don't forget to remove it from BOTH.  I accomplished that with the following bash script (my app is called lanki):

```
# Create the .buildozer folder and download its subcomponents, including the blacklist.txt file we are about to modify
buildozer android update

# Remove the line containing csv so the csv module is installed, as per the warning on
# http://python-for-android.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage/#step-2-package-your-application
# grep -v reverses the search, showing all lines except the one matching the pattern

cwd=$(pwd)

# INSTALL CSV module
# This requires removing _csv from the two blacklist files
# According to
# https://groups.google.com/forum/#!msg/kivy-users/mgisdssF39M/28vgOWfM0y0J
# There are 2 locations for blacklist.txt:
# - one is inside .buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/src/ and 
# - the other is in python-for-android/dist/distname
# the first changes the blacklist for every compilation;
# the second for a specific compilation.
cd .buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/src/
cat blacklist.txt | grep -v "_csv" > blacklist.txt2
rm blacklist.txt
mv blacklist.txt2 blacklist.txt

cd $cwd

cd .buildozer/android/platform/python-for-android/dist/lanki/
cat blacklist.txt | grep -v "_csv" > blacklist.txt2
rm blacklist.txt
mv blacklist.txt2 blacklist.txt

cd $cwd
```

I think I’ve got it. The problem is not Windows-specific, but happens when you don’t have any `python` command available in `PATH` at all. Un\*x systems typically always have a system-wide `python`, which is likely why no-one found out until now.

Pipfile is designed _specifically_ to avoid including these information. Not every application is a package, and Pipenv is designed to handle the  There are tools that can handle this perfectly, such as [flit](https://github.com/takluyver/flit), [pbr](https://docs.openstack.org/pbr/latest/). You can add your package dependencies there (instead of Pipfile), and run `pipenv install -e .` against your package to make Pipenv pick up the dependencies listed there.

Another point to consider is that, not every application has the same dependencies when used as a standalone application and an installable library. Pipenv draws a very clear line here, and does not assume that package requirements are the same as abstract application requirements. See various discussions on this topic, #209 #1161 #1263 etc.

Pipfile is actually an easy target to interpolate against. Pipenv does not care what sections you put inside it (it only reads what it needs, and keep the rest intact). Also, most of Pipenv’s interaction with Pipfile is provided by a standalone library ([sarugaku/requirementslib](https://github.com/sarugaku/requirementslib)). You should be able to build a standalone library to provide the packaging features you need, without adding anything to Pipenv.

To conclude, Pipenv does not actively provide packaging functionalities, and leaves this to other tools. We do strongly encourage people to build tools to interact with Pipfile, and to work alongside with Pipenv. We’d be more than happy to introduce the tool to others as well, if you build it 🤞 

I’m closing this since the proposed functionality is out of the scope of this project, but please do feel free to ask if you need any help on the project (I for one would be interested in contributing, shoot me with an email and we can discuss), or when you want to inform us about any progress you’ve made. Good luck!

I confirm what @fkrull just said. 

The executable string from distutils `build_scripts` command is being split using shlex inside easy_install's [CommandSpec.from_string](https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/master/setuptools/command/easy_install.py#L1966).

Passing `[exec_param]` as a one-element list (when not None) solves the issue, as `CommandSpec._render` method will then concatenate the strings with `subprocess.list2cmdline`, which in turn correctly escapes spaces with double quotes following MS rules.

The `modelsim.vsim_flags` refers to the additional flags you put after the `vsim` command on the same line. Like the `add` is a flag/arguments to the `vcd` command. What you are doing manually in the GUI is not adding a flag to the `vsim` command. The `vsim` command is the one that initialized the simulation and set the top level test bench. 

What you are looking for is likely the `modelsim.init_file.gui` or `modelsim.init_files.after_load` simulation options which you can read about here http://vunit.github.io/python_interface.html?highlight=init_file#simulation-options. They allow you to run any TCL-script you want after the `vsim` command has been executed. FYI the stuff you write in `.do` files and in the modelsim command prompt is just TCL-scripting.

Yes it seems that the problem is due to this environment being created with an older version of conda, as the metadata is missing more than a few fields that are expected in more recent versions. Doing the clone in --offline mode means it doesn't have the data it needs.

A highly related issue occurs if two parallel jobs source activate at the same time in row 609 of install.py:
```
if os.path.lexists(prefix_file):
                os.remove(prefix_file)
```

the process that is "slower" in removing the symlink, dies because there is no specific handling of errno.ENOENT.

In my application I can overcome this by adding an elif for errno.ENOENT:

```
try:
            # try to kill stale links if they exist
            if os.path.lexists(prefix_file):
                os.remove(prefix_file)
            # if they're in use, they won't be killed.  Skip making new symlink.
            if not os.path.lexists(prefix_file):
                symlink_fn(root_file, prefix_file)
        except (IOError, OSError) as e:
            if (os.path.lexists(prefix_file) and
                    (e.errno in (errno.EPERM, errno.EACCES, errno.EROFS, errno.EEXIST))):
                log.debug("Cannot symlink {0} to {1}. Ignoring since link already exists.".format(root_file, prefix_file))
            elif (e.errno in (errno.ENOENT)):
                log.debug("Problem with symlink management {0} {1}. File might have been removed by another process in the meantime." .format(root_file, prefix_file))
            else:
                raise

```

To explain this just a bit more:

PYTHONPATH is for putting your modules in a place so that python knows how to find them.  Some people use it to keep all their one-off scripts in one place, and import them.

PYTHONPATH causes problems when it includes packages that Anaconda or any other Python distribution you have installed on your system also includes.  Modules are found in PYTHONPATH first, before they get found in that distribution's site-packages folder.  You end up with the wrong version imported, and things break.

Many, many people (like you) put site-packages in PYTHONPATH to try to force a particular package to get picked up.  That generally breaks things in unpredictable ways.  It is best to, like Ilan says, unset PYTHONPATH, unless you are completely certain that you will be importing ONLY your code, and not any installed packages from anywhere.  It's safer to simply unset PYTHONPATH.

Each Python process creates its search path on startup.  You can see this with 

```import sys; print(sys.path)```  

If you have PYTHONPATH set, you'll see that its entries appear at the beginning of sys.path - this is why modules in the first folder clobber others.  If you don't have PYTHONPATH set, then nothing else gets in the way - only the site-packages folder local to the current python interpreter will get found.

This is completely separate from which python interpreter gets found.  PATH is the environment variable that determines that.  Conda's activate script takes care of setting PATH so that the desired interpreter in your env gets found.

`python=3.6.1-2` is not the correct spec. It's either `python=3.6.1=2` or `python 3.6.1 2`, depending on how you've implemented it. `python=3.6.1-2` means `Python version 3.6.1-2`, not `Python version 3.6.1 build 2`.

> Then there are those who say that conda should never ever look in `user_site`

I personally have a foot there too.  And I'd go so far as saying this should be the default for any conda-installed python (given that any conda-install python will by definition *always* be in a conda environment).  The environment variable I'd propose then would be to open up the interpreter to look in `user_site` if people actually want that behavior.

That's my personal opinion though, and why this issue is marked as a discussion topic.  If we make any type of change here, we need to make sure it's thoroughly discussed and we have consensus on the behavior change.

Sorry I forgot to give a feedback but here's my conclusion to get a working build:

- CMake 3.0 is broken and does not pass -std c++11 correctly to G++, using 3.5 from backports fix this. I think that's the cause of the original report: "error: identifier "nullptr" is undefined"
- There's something wrong with old GCC and CUDA NVCC (which calls g++): I patched you CMake file to force addition of -D_FORCE_INLINES (that's the one causing syntax errors when building a CUDA variant)
```
--- mxnet-0.12.0+debian.orig/mshadow/cmake/Cuda.cmake
+++ mxnet-0.12.0+debian/mshadow/cmake/Cuda.cmake
@@ -165,7 +165,7 @@ macro(mshadow_cuda_compile objlist_varia
 
   endforeach()
   if(UNIX OR APPLE)
-    list(APPEND CUDA_NVCC_FLAGS -Xcompiler -fPIC)
+    list(APPEND CUDA_NVCC_FLAGS -Xcompiler -fPIC -D_FORCE_INLINES)
   endif()
 
   if(APPLE)
```
- There's something broken on Debian Jessie: CUDA 7.5 from backports does not depends on libthrust-dev from backports. That's the one causing "thrust::system::cuda::detail::par_t" has no member "on"

@reminisce Regarding your question about the cycle check, I'd like to juxtapose two approaches, one of which is the one taken here, and the other is more typical but probably less efficient for large graphs.

Here's your graph (starred nodes are TensorRT-compatible, unstarred ones are not):

A* →   D
↓        ↓
B* →  C*


Approach 1 - naively growing the subgraph and checking for cycles each time

This approach tests adding a new node and if a cycle is formed, the node is rejected. Taking your graph as an example, say we start with A*. Clearly TensorRT only helps when there are 2 or more nodes, due to its benefit being mainly due to fusion. So, we get (A*, B*) in the set of nodes in the TensorRT graph. This is fine, there's no cycle, because the edge is from B* to C*, not the other way around. Now we try adding C*, to get (A*, B*, C*) in the TensorRT subgraph. Let's call this subgraph T*. Now D is the only node outside the subgraph and we have a cycle from T* to D, back to T*. This was an illegal contraction, so the last step is rejected, and only A* → B* gets substituted with T*. This is in fact how TensorRT integration with TensorFlow works, or used to, the last time I checked.


Approach 2 - two search passes, examining sets of pairs

Instead of the naive subgraph growth, let's enumerate all pairs of nodes that are TensorRT-compatible or incompatible. A pair compatible if both nodes between the edge are TensorRT-compatible - only then can they go into the TensorRT subgraph. 

For the graph above, this would be A* → B* and B* → C*.

A* → D is incompatible because D is incompatible, so the whole pair is incompatible.
Similarly, D → C* is incompatible because D is incompatible.

However, if there is a node which is part of both a compatible pair and an incompatible pair, it's no longer a candidate for the TensorRT graph. Such a node is A*. That's because even though it's compatible in one pair (A* → B*), it's incompatible in another (A* → D). So, the only edge that can be collapsed is B* → C* and these two nodes become T*. Now the graph after the rewrite will look as follows:

A*  →   D 
 ↘  T* ↙


The graph partitioner for this commit takes Approach 2. We will add a unit test for this to show that it works.

I hope this clarifies your question as to how we handle cycles.

Agreed that we shouldn't repeat ourselves. How about this (a restatement of our conclusions from yesterday plus some new thoughts):

## SDK

- In the SDK we are committed to preventing requests that will fail :100: because of invalid arguments (image sizes, travel modes, geocoding datasets)
- Let's validate them using functions in a `validation` module, not with in-line code.
- Raise SDK-specific exceptions that derive from `ValueError` (or `KeyError` for, say, travel modes).

## CLI

- We're committed :100: to sparing CLI users from reading tracebacks.
- The CLI takes responsibility for validating: input/output file paths and the few other things that aren't a concern of the SDK
- Otherwise, rely on the SDK, re-raising its errors as you suggest above.
- We could reserve the option to write validiating Click callbacks using methods from the SDK's validation module if we have a future need.

Just wanted to let you know that I've been using pyfftw and mpi4py to do FFTs in parallel with MPI (http://arxiv.org/abs/1602.03638), with thousands of processors on a Blue Gene supercomputer. Using pyfftw to do the serial FFT and then mpi4py to do the required MPI communications does not really require much work and if you are already working in Python, there's really no good reason why you should have to wrap the FFTWs MPI routines. In fact, four lines of compact Python code is all you need to execute an FFT with slab decomposition, which is exactly what FFTW does. I've implemented it here: https://github.com/spectralDNS/mpiFFT4py. Since I'm a researcher the code is tailored to my needs, which is a pseudo-spectral direct numerical simulation code for turbulence, but perhaps it can be of use to you as well?

Best regards

Mikael

As to:
> - Now there's `array<safe>` and `array<unsafe>` which are different types from C++'s point of view, although really they are the same thing. Also this template parameter is meaningless from return type perspective
I think they should be convertible, i.e. I'm pretty sure you'll be able to do: `array<unsafe> aunsafe = asafe;` and have two (C++) variables referencing the same ndarray, but one checks on access while the other doesn't.
(This could be something like a filter over ``Extras`` that assembles a ``std::tuple`` with all of the call guards)

Turning this into a static local variable doesn't seem like such a nice solution because it comes with various extra baggage. (C++ will defer initialization until the function is called and will add locking code to make the initialization thread-safe).

It's the same story for some of the other format() functions like ``npy_format_descriptor<char[N]>``.

It seems to me that the better way to go would be just to return ``std::string`` everywhere to account for the fact that these strings may be dynamically generated.

Hi @drozzy,

uuid1 has 14 bits of randomness plus a mac address (called the "node").
Collisions shouldn't not occur between uuids generated by multiple scrapyd instances.
However, If you permanently store the jobids in a table, you will have collisions for the same nodes.
We definitely need more bits of randomness than bits for the node.

So, it makes sense to accept this change.
Let's include it in the next release.

The only possible concern is users decoding the node part of the uuid
to get the node on which the job was ran — this will break such functionality.
We should include it in a changes section in the changelog.

I'll now add a 1.2 changelog in master documenting changes in 1.2.0a1.
Then you can rebase on master, add a line referring to using uuid4 instead of uuid1
and I'll merge.

@murphp15 Multi-headed attention has the same method declaration, but so does any torch `Module` - it's just `forward`.  The issue is that the expected input and output shapes of the tensors do not match.  We define these `Registrable` abstractions on things that are interchangeable in a model without requiring any code change.  For that to work, the tensors that the modules take as input and the tensors they return as output have to have identical shapes - otherwise, when you switch to a different implementation of the same `Registrable` object, your code breaks, because something is getting a differently-shaped tensor than it was expecting.

`MatrixAttention` defines its output as having shape `(batch_size, num_rows_1, num_rows_2)`.  The multi-headed attention function actually returns something that has shape `(batch_size, num_rows_1, num_rows_2, num_heads)`.  This breaks the abstraction.

2 Reasons:
1. You Label Vocabulary should be {0 'positive', 1: 'negative'}
It seems that you have also changed the namespace of vocabulary for label field. By default, if you keep you namespace anything ending with tag or label, then it will not add any @@PADDING@@ and @@UNKNOWN@@ token to that namespace. If you choose to use something else, then you must explicitly say that it is non_padded in your experiment.json
```
  "vocabulary": {
    "non_padded_namespaces": ["my_namespace"]
  },
```
You probably set this namespace in your datasetreader: 
```
fields['my_namespace'] = LabelField(label)
```
If you used the provided snli reader, it shouldn't be a problem since it uses `fields['label'] = LabelField(label)`

2. Your error says 'final output dimension must match number of labels, but got **3** and **4** instead'. After you fix above, it will expect 2. Your code is generating 3 logits probably because of you didn't change the final aggregation neural network. SNLI by default has 3 labels and so default is to generate 3 logits:

```
    "aggregate_feedforward": {
      "input_dim": 400,
      "num_layers": 2,
      "hidden_dims": [200, 3],        # Change 3 to number of classes you want to predict. 2 in your case.
      "activations": ["relu", "linear"],
      "dropout": [0.2, 0.0]
    },
```

@astrofrog - What would be your preference to set this up and maintain it for `regions`. Do you want to commit the PNGs in the repo?

My first preference would be to only store a text file.
My second preference would be many text files.
My least favourite solution is many PNGs or FITS files.
(that said, I'm still OK with that last solution, if it's your preference)

In my experience what happens is this: the reference results are committed, and remain valid for a year. Then there's a code change or dependency change, and a bug report or pull request where this becomes relevant again. Usually what I do is go to the first pixel that has a difference and try to understand why it changed and if the old or new value is correct. I.e. I prefer to have the text file in the repo. Yes, having PNGs or FITS to quickly inspect things can sometimes be nice, but they can always be generated locally from the text files, I don't think they are useful enough to be stored in the repo.

The 'best' solution that i found is to manually create an [AWS::IAM::Role](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-resource-iam-role.html).
##### cloudformation.yaml
```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: 2010-09-09
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Resources:
    Function:
        Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
        Properties:
            Runtime: python2.7
            CodeUri: lambda.zip
            Handler: lambda.handler
            Role: !GetAtt Role.Arn
    Role:
        Type: AWS::IAM::Role
        Properties:
            AssumeRolePolicyDocument:
                Version: 2012-10-17
                Statement:
                    Effect: Allow
                    Principal:
                        Service: lambda.amazonaws.com
                    Action: sts:AssumeRole
            ManagedPolicyArns:
                - arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
                - !ImportValue TopicPolicyARN

```

This does not discount an issue with your authoritative DNS servers. We make unboundtest.com available specifically because it emulates the same recursive resolver settings Let's Encrypt uses in production (0x20 case randomization, fully validating DNSSEC, etc).

> Also, if you ping it on any Windows or Linux system, you'll see DNS resolves,

It seems a bit worrisome that we would officially bless Python packages as the temporary solution for 4.x nbextensions, and then turn 180º and say that it's all npm, and not Python packages as fast as we can. But if we want to make it more convenient, adding a flag for 'install js from a Python package':

    jupyter nbextension install --py nbgrader

seems like a better middle ground than breaking `jupyter nbextension install [path]`, or guessing whether `[path]` is a path or a package.

> Do we need separate config=True traitlets on NotebookApp for enabling common, notebook, tree and terminal nbextensions?

I don't think so. What would these common nbextensions be?

> How do we handle multiple config files that define those? Last wins? Try to merge?

I would do it the same way we do with the rest of config, where the more specific config has priority: user > env > system. I think that's the only thing that's missing from nbconfig for this to work.

> How will folks at Continuum write these config files programmatically?

During the nbextension discussion a year ago, it was concluded that we must not activate extensions as part of installing them - that install & activate **must** be two separate actions. Are we changing our minds on that?

If someone wants to *enable* nbextensions via conda packages, the biggest hurdle is that all enabling currently resides in a single file, and conda packages should only write, not modify files. To support this, the only idea I have is a `config.d`-style directory of config files, so that packages could drop a file in there, and all such files are loaded.

Hi,

So, one of the things I don't like that much about OpenCV compared to PIL or accimage is that OpenCV returns images in a different format than PIL (BGR instead of RGB), and we'd like for consistency to have all our operations return 0-1 RGB images.

We could patch in some sense `ToTensor` to take that into account (using the `image_backend` option), but given that OpenCV returns numpy arrays, I'm not sure how robust such solution would be, given that we already handle numpy arrays in [`ToTensor`](https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/master/torchvision/transforms/functional.py#L46-L53), and the lingua-franca that we use for the input/output of most of our transforms is PIL images.

I'm afraid having to backends with different data representations might lead to hard to spot bugs in the user code if we don't do things carefully.
I'm open to suggestions.

> But do we need to store invisible params into history and\or allow to access them from remote scheduler?
Yes. We do have some such params in our tasks where machine cares about the params but humans don't. One example is a param called `number_of_chunks` which is used  to split a task into smaller chunks in map -> reduce scenario. The param is redundant because it's visible from the DAG anyway. Another example has to do with proxying params. There is an intermediary step that doesn't use the params itself but proxies them from downstream to upsteam. Again, we don't need to see most of those params on the task but they are crucial for proper functioning.

I like your idea of not concerning ourselves with what a directory will consider an equivalent filter. It simplifies much of this, and makes sense since this library shouldn't be trying to anticipate the needs of many different directories.

With that being said, I agree that it would then make sense to avoid the escaping function when considering equivalence. We'll only consider the attribute and value when checking for equivalence and not the escaped/encoded value. I'll make that change.

Further, you are also correct that I'm only trying to find equivalences between simple AND and ORs, not complex logical equivalents, e.g. DeMorgan's. I'll save solving [boolean satisfiability](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Boolean_satisfiability_problem) for another day :)

To your last point, I do particularly like using `__eq__`. It makes comparison Just Work™ with different libraries and operations. For example, [assertEqual](https://docs.python.org/2/library/unittest.html#unittest.TestCase.assertEqual) and other functions that use `==` will just work without having to add special comparators.

I did briefly look into why different escapers were resulting in the same filter. I would've expected otherwise given the predicate `asText`. I did come up with a test to prove your point that there could be incorrect results, I'll add it to the PR. To remedy the issue I've changed to using `str` instead of `asText`. Good catch!

So I've:

* Added some comparison optimizations
* Changed the semantics to not rely on `asText` and thus not rely on the escaper
* Kept `__eq__`, I fairly partial to using it, let me know if this is a dealbreak

Let me know what you think!

I don't really see a use case for passing to controllers. Headers are to be picked up by the infrastructure (gateways and the framework), usually. I realize you can specify `in: header` according to the docs, but that doesn't necessarily mean they should be passed to controllers. They *should* be validated and type casted though.

The Swagger spec is IMO a bit vague on header params. I mean this (from the spec):

```yml
name: token
in: header
description: token to be passed as a header
required: true
type: array
items:
  type: integer
  format: int64
collectionFormat: csv
``` 

Is fine, except for the name. Are we not using the "X-" extension prefix for HTTP header names? Dash-separated-upper-case-first? *Or* are we leaving out a transformation from header name to parameter name? In the latter case the interface would be incomplete so we have to assume the former. So we're talking:

```yml
name: X-Token
in: header
description: token to be passed as a header
required: true
type: array
items:
  type: integer
  format: int64
collectionFormat: csv
``` 

So we could strip `X-`, convert the rest to snake-case and pass it to the function. But what you probably want is to have it picked up by some middleware (eg Flask decorators), instead.

To summarize: yes to validate and cast, but using the result in a decorator would most likely be much more useful than as an argument.

Do you mean #242? If so that's OpenSUSE, not FreeBSD.

``$DESTDIR`` is not a FreeBSD specific thing anyway, this is something that is generally expected of **all build systems** under non-windows. If it works under Linux it's very likely that it will also work under UNIX like operating systems.

``$DESTDIR`` only matters if you set it and its not set by default so the installation locations are not changed from where were before this PR. The only real change for the installation locations in this PR is that the python site-packages directory must now live somewhere underneath the prefix.

Anyway, if you really care about building on these platforms why don't you have continuous integration builds set up for them (even better, have them build PRs and have them be publicly viewable)? It is not realistic to expect every contributor to test their changes under many platforms because

* They might not have access to all of the platforms you care about.
* Even if they do have access they might have the time to run on all the different platforms.

when match is hit, if there's another acceptable match, remove the item from the list and dont reinsert it

I started work on this already and had a plan in progress that might be worth considering. The first step was to convert all `printf` calls to use the `log_xxx` macros in libswiftnav so the meaning of each message was captured. The `log_xxx` macros were just stubbed out to call `printf` so that this step could be completed independently of the changes to the protocol. This step is largely complete although there are still a couple `printfs` still in libswiftnav.

The second step was to change the `log_xxx` macros to point to a logging function (`log()`) that takes a string and a log level. A default implementation (e.g. `_log_default()`) would be provided in libswiftnav that calls `printf` but would be weak aliased to `log`. This means that is the user of the library provides an implementation of `log` it will override the default, otherwise the default will be linked in. Then we can implement `log` in piksi_firmware that then hooks into a new message format.

Also, I'm not sure where all this stuff about newline termination came from. `MSG_PRINT` is not newline terminated and never has been. There are many places in the firmware where messages are output without a newline. The newline is just to indicate, well, a newline.

In general I don't think that this suggestion will work satisfactorily with ttfautohint.  The idea of using the components' hints only by default is based on the 'base glyph plus diacritics' model where the components don't interact with the base glyph of the composite.  In particular, you get only satisfactory results if the `ROUND_XY_TO_GRID` composite flag is set – if you are going to transform components non-trivially, I suppose this flag is normally *not* set (since you can't reliably connect glyph shapes of components otherwise) and you have to use ttfautohint's `--composite` command line option.

Another issue is that `--composite` makes ttfautohint internally decompose composite glyphs, and suddenly *all* components of the glyph take part in the hinting algorithm, which can lead to differently positioned stems or composites.  Doing this for some glyphs only might thus yield visually bad results.

Mixing up the two 'modes' would make sense, of course.  For example, a composite's base glyph might be itself a composite that doesn't use `ROUND_XY_TO_GRID`.  However, this is something that ttfautohint can't handle automatically – how should it know which is which?  Theoretically, I could add commands to ttfautohint's control instructions file that enable this feature manually.  However, I wonder whether there are enough use cases for it.

So I can think of two ways to accomplish this in the current toolset, they're not the greatest but maybe they get you going in the right direction?

The `SKIP` environment variable allows you to temporarily disable hooks, so if I didn't want to run `autopep8` or `flake8` on an invocation I could run `SKIP=flake8,autopep8 pre-commit`. http://pre-commit.com/#temporarily-disabling-hooks

pre-commit can run individual hooks at a time by passing them on the commandline: `pre-commit run trailing-whitespace`.  You could do something where you maybe `echo 'trailing-whitespace flake8' | xargs -n1 pre-commit run`? (briefly mentioned here: http://pre-commit.com/#usage)

A tangentially related feature: you can already configure hooks to run at different stages (currently only pre-commit / pre-push) via http://pre-commit.com/#confining-hooks-to-run-at-certain-stages

As for implementing I'm a little concerned for a couple reasons:
- you can _kinda_ do it without adding additional tooling (yeah it's not the best, but it is possible)
- I'm not sure if the feature will get much use via the main usage of the tool: as a pre-commit hook (I'm not even sure how it would work with that, perhaps writing the configuration to  the .git/hooks/pre-commit executable, but then it's not idempotent any more and that complicates as well).
- at least for our usage we're running every hook every time on every commit

Actually I think sending a single test item works without a problem, IIRC I have tested this in the past because I faced the same use case, where we had a few slow tests.

What @RonnyPfannschmidt is mentioning is actually on the slave side, where each slave will always have at least one test "on queue", until it receives more tests from the master node or a `"shutdown"` signal from master. This happens because the slave must comply with the [pytest_runtest_teardown(item, nextitem)](http://pytest.org/latest/writing_plugins.html?highlight=hooks#_pytest.hookspec.pytest_runtest_teardown) hook: before calling it it must know if there is a `nextitem` or if the last test on queue is the last test in the session, in which case it should use `nextitem=None`. Until the slave receives more tests or a `"shutdown"` signal, it has no way of knowing which is which, that's why it will keep at least one test waiting ([here's](https://github.com/pytest-dev/pytest-xdist/blob/master/xdist/remote.py#L57) the relevant code).

```
-   "version": "3.6.2"
```

I disagree with Yury on this one. (Not sure if current-Yury agrees with 2016-Yury either, that was quite some time ago...)

Trio follows the rule that all you need to know about `await` is that there are async functions and regular functions, and async functions are called like `await some_func()` and regular functions are called like `some_func()`. In fact, the actual `__await__` protocol allows for all kinds of more complicated things – you can `await` objects, you can call async functions without `await` – but we intentionally don't do any of that in order to keep things simple and understandable.

So for example, `open_nursery` is a sync function that returns an async context manager object, so you can use it like `async with open_nursery()`. `open_file` and `open_tcp_stream` are async functions that return trio file and trio stream objects, respectively, so you can use them like `await open_file(...)`, `await open_tcp_stream(...)`. It happens that trio file and trio stream objects both implement the async context manager protocol. So, it follows that you can write `async with await open_file(...)` or `async with await open_tcp_stream(...)`.

But to make both `f = await open_file(...)` work *and* have `async with open_file(...)` work, we'd have to make `open_file` some sort of hybrid function that's sorta-async and sorta-sync and shouldn't even be possible according to the simplified model we teach people in the tutorial. So... we don't do that.

We should fix that example though.

Ah, sure, ok.  Looking at the code what I'd actually suggest is the same paradigm that `numpy` uses:
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/master/numpy/lib/npyio.py#L473
Basically, if `isinstance(path, six.string_types) = True` you `open` the file for reading or writing as `fid` and set a flag `own_fid` to `True`, then if `own_fid` is `True` you `fid.close()` at the end of the function.  If `isinstance(path, six.string_types) = False` you treat it like a file pointer, and don't `open` or `close` anything.  This is instead of using `path.name` and the `with open` context.  If you'd like, I can implement this.  I did something similar here: https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/io.py#L51

OK, what we have here is a disagreement between the Toil way of doing
things (where you import a URL) and the IOStore way of doing things (where
you can only import a local file path).

I think the right way to do things is going to be to import to the
fileStore, and then to immediately download the file from the fileStore and
stick it in the IOStore if we're also supposed to be dumping to the
IOStore. People are going to want to be able to use input data in, say, S3,
while still dumping to the IOStore.

Hi @aarora8,

Please see #3009 and http://scikit-image.org/docs/dev/user_guide/data_types.html

What is happening here is that your input image has dtype int32/uint32 (you ensure this by setting `dtype=int` in your padding function), and it is getting rescaled by the conversion to float inside `rotate`. As I mention in #3009, I think this is not the correct policy, and we plan to fix it (somehow).

In the meantime, you can probably set `dtype=image.dtype` in your `pad` function, and things should work as expected.

I would also point you to the `np.pad` function, which does what you need, probably more efficiently, too. And `max_val * np.ones` can be replaced by `np.full`. =)

I'm going to close this issue because it is covered by #3009. We will work on a fix there. Please feel free to ask further questions here if my response isn't clear!

```
from scipy.fftpack import fft, ifft, fftfreq

from skimage.io import imread
from skimage import data_dir
from skimage.transform import radon, rescale

freqcutoff = 1# 0.3

image = rescale(image, scale=0.4)
theta = np.linspace(0., 180., max(image.shape), endpoint=False)
sinogram = radon(image, theta=theta, circle=True)

projection_size_padded =  max(64, int(2 ** np.ceil(np.log2(2 * sinogram.shape[0]))))

# Construct the Fourier filter
f = fftfreq(projection_size_padded).reshape(-1, 1)#*freqcutoff   # digital frequency   
omega = 2 * np.pi * f                                # angular frequency
fs = np.fft.ifftshift(f)

plt.close('all')
fig1 = plt.figure(1)
ax1 = fig1.add_subplot(111)
fig2 = plt.figure(2)

fourier_filter_rl[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax1.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_rl))
######### new # the same as old in this case
fourier_filter_rl2 = 2 * np.abs(f)         # ramp filter
fourier_filter_rl2[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax2.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_rl2))

fourier_filter_sl = 2 * np.abs(f) 
# Start from first element to avoid divide by zero
fourier_filter_sl[1:] = fourier_filter_sl[1:] * (np.sin(omega[1:]/(freqcutoff)) / (omega[1:]/(freqcutoff)))
fourier_filter_sl[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax1.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_sl))
######### new
fourier_filter_sl2 = 2 * np.abs(f) 
# Start from first element to avoid divide by zero
fourier_filter_sl2[1:] = fourier_filter_sl2[1:] * (np.sin(omega[1:]/(2*freqcutoff)) / (omega[1:]/(2*freqcutoff)))
fourier_filter_sl2[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax2.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_sl2))

# plot cosine filter
######### old
fourier_filter_cos = 2 * np.abs(f) 
fourier_filter_cos *= np.cos(omega/(freqcutoff))
fourier_filter_cos[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax1.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_cos))
######### new 
fourier_filter_cos2 = 2 * np.abs(f) 
fourier_filter_cos2 *= np.cos(omega/(2*freqcutoff))
fourier_filter_cos2[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax2.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_cos2))

# plot hamming filter
######### old
fourier_filter_hamm = 2 * np.abs(f) 
fourier_filter_hamm *= (0.54 + 0.46 * np.cos(omega / (2*freqcutoff)))
fourier_filter_hamm[np.where(2*np.abs(f)>freqcutoff)]=0
ax1.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_hamm))

fourier_filter_hamm2 *= (0.54 + 0.46 * np.cos(omega / (freqcutoff)))

ax2.plot(fs,np.fft.ifftshift(fourier_filter_hann2))

ax1.set_title('Old version')
ax1.set_xlabel('Frequency')
ax1.set_ylabel('Filter')
ax1.axis('tight')
ax1.legend(['Ram-Lak','Shepp-Logan','Cosine','Hamming','Hanning'],loc='best')
ax2.set_title('New version')
```

```python
from skimage import data, exposure, filters, color, morphology, io
import skimage
from skimage.measure import label
from PIL import Image
import numpy as np
np.set_printoptions(threshold='nan')
# camera = data.camera()

# print camera.shape

def im2double(im):
    min_val = np.min(im.ravel())
    max_val = np.max(im.ravel())
    out = (im.astype('float') - min_val) / (max_val - min_val)
    return out

def imbinarize(img, threshold):
    img[img >= threshold] = 1
    img[img < threshold] = 0
    return img

img = Image.open("building.jpg")
img = img.convert('RGB')
img_data = np.array(img)
double_img = skimage.img_as_float64(img_data)

hsv_img = color.rgb2hsv(img_data)
H = hsv_img[:, :, 0]
S = hsv_img[:, :, 1]
V = hsv_img[:, :, 2]
NDI = (S - V)/(S + V)

NDI[np.argwhere(np.isnan(NDI))] = 0	# prevent NaN

level = filters.threshold_otsu(NDI)
binary = imbinarize(NDI, level)
labelled = label(binary, connectivity=1) 
selem = morphology.square(3)
dilated = morphology.binary_dilation(labelled, selem)
sub = dilated.astype(np.float32) - labelled.astype(np.float32)

var_binary = np.var(sub)
var_labelled = np.var(labelled)
mean_binary = np.mean(sub)
mean_labelled = np.mean(labelled)
modified_img = mean_binary + ((labelled - mean_labelled)/var_binary)*var_labelled

final_img_data = im2double(modified_img)
final_img = Image.fromarray(final_img_data, "L")
final_img.show()

```

You won't fix any _credential caching behaviour_, because calling `ztoken` with no parameters won't cache the token. You will get a new token when calling it again.

What you can do is use a named token, which returns the same token if already created (and not expired), as in `ztoken token -n mytoken`. And since the token is valid for any forked process, it should be anyway executed before the fork and only once, so I still don't see a valid enough reason to make it concurrent safe :smirk:

@escoand 
> So, what is the best way to represent multi-service devices? As far as I know there is always only one port per device.

As for `multi-service` devices - per bonjour printing spec (and in reality ;)), devices are allowed to publish as many print services via bonojur as supported by device using corresponding ports. So physical device can use several ports (not one). Devices are required to set priority to each service type which again per bonjour spec should be reported in TXT record (`priority` TXT record field).

> What do you think about escoand/netdisco@e5c7560

This looks as generic way to discover standard print service types which should work for any vendor. i.e it opens road for many other components - vendor specific and not (possibly custom as well?).

If purpose of your discovery is to detect that there is printing device in general present or not present (i.e the fact that bonjour service has been discovered can be treated as `become online` event and when bonjour service has disappeared can be treated as `became offline` event assuming that bonjour discovery is endless process) - then strategy to discover all possible Bonjour service types looks very reasonable as for me because it allows to cover the maximum of device models from any vendors. However, if you need to make sure that only unique devices were reported then you have to gather all discovered service types and match them all by service name in order to identify that they belong to single device and process event (online/offline) after that matching only.
From other hand you can just select single bonjour service type for this purpose and rely only on it to monitor offline/online state of printer and it would cover probably really big amount of devices/vendors.

Actually, it might be responsibility of specific component (platform) outside of discovery to consider matching or if service type is applicable or valuable for it. Another consideration is just a question of your design preferences - what you want to represent in hass - services or device which provides them (this actually not ends with print services, scan and fax can be published by aio device).
Hope this helps rather then misleads :)

I would tend to consider basic traversing (`ITraversable `) to be an important part of why zope.container exists, so a dependency on `zope.traversing` seems warranted to me. 

I don't feel the same way about `IBrowserPublisher` and `IXMLRPCPublisher` and wouldn't object to them being factored out, but then again, I don't use them or `zope.publisher`, so I'm biased there.

On the gripping hand, though, those two projects don't need a C compiler and don't pull in many (if any) dependencies that aren't already included in `zope.container`, so from my perspective the benefits of splitting out an additional library are very small, perhaps small enough to be bumping into the law of diminishing returns.

So I guess I would be -1 for removing `zope.traversing` support and -0 for removing `zope.publisher` support.


This is explained by the following:
```python
from invenio_records.api import get_record
from invenio.ext.sqlalchemy import db
r = get_record(1)

db.session.dirty == IdentitySet([])
r['cat'] = 'dog'
db.session.dirty == IdentitySet([<invenio_records.models.RecordMetadata object at 0x7fe09f116310>])
```
In other words assigning to the key leaves a lingering transaction, that `commit` inside the API's `commit` does not cover.

This trasaction happens here:

It is important to know that on line `--> 267             self._dict[key] = value` (stack level 3 above), `self._dict` is a `<class 'sqlalchemy.ext.mutable.MutableDict'>`. I'm actually looking at the inverse of this, where I want to retrieve an existing foreign key when it already exists when posted nested data (as opposed to sending it in as you're doing).  In my case, a user might send just the name field without knowing the unique ID.  Probably bad design but I'm new at this.

I don't know if this helps, but I did make some progress on this via the get_unique method detailed here:  http://stackoverflow.com/questions/24290858/insert-or-update-duplicate-key-in-sqlalchemy-integrityerror

I just add a pre_load decorator to my schemas to map the instance if it exists:

```
@pre_load
def get_unique(self, data):
    self.instance = self.opts.model.get_unique(**data)
```

This works great for the primary or parent object, but related objects nested in the posted JSON still throw integrity errors.  I haven't figured out exactly how MA-SQLA builds related model objects, but if the get_unique method could be implemented when building related models, I think that might solve your issue as well.  IE, if a foreign key/field is passed, if it exists it would be mapped, otherwise it could be created.  Or maybe I'm misreading things.

Again, maybe this is horrible design or concept, but for my purposes, being able to generate and POST a complex, nested JSON object and have it automagically (map existing/create new) nested/related table data would be a huge benefit for building APIs.

When people use a namespace, they typically (in my experience, an overwhelming majority of the time) do it for an entire feature / section of a codebase, so

```
d = Dataset('foo', namespace='bar')
```

would be in one team's production code while

```
d = Dataset('foo', namespace='baz')
```

would be in another's.

This seems much more reasonable than having 3 classes to handle this scenario

```
Client -> Dataset -> Namespace
```

This is due to known, unchangeable behavior in the way gRPC handles message buffers. I'm working on providing documentation that describes exactly how this occurs and ways to mitigate it.

While I'd like to imagine my code is bug-free, and I have no specific idea of where a bug might be, the piece of code I'd be by far most suspicious of causing a deadlock is the async `background_thread.py` logging handler transport since it starts a new thread, and both the main thread and background thread acquire locks. I had to fix a few deadlocks when I first wrote it.

I would suggest commenting out `test_log_handler_async` and `test_log_root_handler` and see if these deadlocks still happen. If not, we've narrowed it down quite a bit. We could also switch the default transport to the synchronous option until bugs are ironed out.

I think the log handlers are important and a backgrounded writer is important but it is always tricky to get multi-threaded code right, probably should have marked those in some sort of `contrib` package in first place. We had discussed moving it out of this repo altogether but I think standard logging handlers prob do belong in here, in either case it seems like @duggelz and @liyanhui1228 are building on top of it and taking ownership of some of these sort of features, so maybe they can take a look and help iron out any kinks?

Again, this is pure speculation because I don't actually have any reason to think there are bugs in that code other than its an easy piece of code to have subtle hard-to-find, hard-to-repro bugs in.

Thanks for running the commands! In terms of how to update from the old guest environment, our recommended approach is the one-time update to the newest Ubuntu images. Note that changes to the guest environment will be incremental in the foreseeable future, so package updates should work after this one time update. I would highly suggest modifying your instance group to refer to the image family (if it doesn't already) so that you aren't creating new instances from a potentially deprecated image version.

If you want to update packages in an already running VM, I'll try and give some pointers, but please take a snapshot of your disk so you have a backup in case something goes wrong. Also note that Ubuntu images are managed by Canonical, not Google, so there are likely better ways to do this :)

```
sudo su
ps aux | grep python.*google | awk '{print $2}' | xargs kill -9
apt-get purge --auto-remove gce-daemon gce-startup-scripts
apt-get install gce-compute-image-packages
reboot
```

Hi Daniel,

The code you have been using doesn't work as `clone_from` is a class method. Thus it doesn't know the state of the configured `Repo` instance referred to by `repo`.

Instead what you could do is to use the `env` keyword parameter of `clone_from` such as in this example:

```python
Repo.clone_from(url, dst_path, branch=branch, env=dict(GIT_SSH_COMMAND=ssh_cmd))
```

Last but not least, please be sure you are using git 2.3 or newer, as `GIT_SSH_COMMAND` was only recently introduced. Additional notes about the `GIT_SSH_COMMAND` environment variable can be found in the [git-python docs][gp_docs].

I hope that helps !

[gp_docs]: http://gitpython.readthedocs.org/en/stable/tutorial.html#handling-remotes
